instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: A structured beginning for new employees
Learning everyday judgement from an experienced colleague
Keeping established staff informed about change
Checking whether training improves real performance
Moving all training outside paid working hours
Why online courses will replace every instructor

paragraphs.text: During their first week, new employees follow an induction timetable rather than joining a team without preparation. The programme introduces building safety, essential computer systems, and the people responsible for common requests. Short practical tasks allow staff to use each system with support before they need it in normal work. Managers also explain how the role contributes to the wider organisation. The purpose is not to teach every future duty, but to give newcomers a reliable starting point and reduce avoidable confusion.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes a planned induction that gives new staff a safe and organized start.

paragraphs.text: After induction, each new employee is paired with a colleague who has performed the role for at least a year. The colleague does not deliver formal lessons. Instead, the pair discuss difficult situations, observe how routine decisions are made, and review questions at the end of each week. This arrangement passes on practical knowledge that is rarely written in a manual. It also gives the newcomer a familiar person to approach before a small uncertainty becomes a larger workplace error.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is informal mentoring that transfers practical judgement and gives newcomers support.

paragraphs.text: Training is not limited to recently hired staff. When regulations, software, or customer procedures change, experienced employees attend short refresher sessions. These sessions begin with what has changed rather than repeating the entire original course. Staff then apply the new rule to examples from their own work and identify questions that the written guidance has not answered. Regular updates prevent old habits from continuing after a policy changes and help different teams apply the same standard consistently.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on refresher training that updates experienced staff when policies or tools change.

paragraphs.text: A completed course does not automatically prove that training was useful. Supervisors compare work quality before and after a programme, while participants report which parts they could apply. Customer feedback and error records may offer additional evidence over several months. If performance remains unchanged, the organisation examines whether the material, practice time, or workplace support was inadequate. Evaluation therefore connects training to actual behaviour and allows the next version of a course to address weaknesses rather than simply repeat the same content.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains how workplaces evaluate training through later performance and feedback.

instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: How to join and use the borrowing service
Library resources available beyond the building
Activities that bring local people together
Choosing and reserving a suitable study space
Why overdue fines pay for all library services
Plans to replace the present building

paragraphs.text: Anyone who lives, works, or studies in Greenfield can apply for a library card without charge. Applicants should bring proof of their address or workplace to the main desk. Members may borrow up to twelve items for three weeks, and most loans can be renewed online if another reader has not reserved them. Children under sixteen need a parent or guardian to approve the application. The borrowing rules are explained during registration, so new members can begin using the collection immediately.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph is mainly a guide to membership, registration, and borrowing rules.

paragraphs.text: Membership also provides access to services that do not require a visit to the library. Readers can download electronic books, listen to audiobooks, and search newspaper archives through the library website. A card number and password are needed when connecting from home. Staff run a weekly online help session for anyone unfamiliar with the system. Although digital titles cannot replace every printed item, they allow members to use a substantial part of the collection when the building is closed.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is access to digital library materials and support from outside the building.

paragraphs.text: Greenfield Library hosts events designed around participation rather than quiet individual reading. A conversation group welcomes adults practising English, while a Saturday workshop gives families simple science activities to complete together. Local residents also meet monthly to exchange seeds and gardening advice. Most events are free, though places must be booked when equipment is limited. By offering regular reasons to meet, the library acts as a community space where people can share skills as well as borrow information.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph highlights participatory events that connect and involve community members.

paragraphs.text: Visitors who need a quiet place can choose between open desks and six small study rooms. The rooms are intended for groups of two to six people and may be reserved for a maximum of two hours. Individual readers should use the silent area upstairs, where phone calls and group discussion are not permitted. A display near the entrance shows which spaces are free. These arrangements help visitors select an area that matches the type of work they plan to do.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains the different study spaces and how visitors can select or reserve them.

instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: Respectful use of areas shared by all residents
Reporting repairs and understanding who must act
Reducing disturbance from noise and visitors
Simple systems for waste and lower energy use
The annual process for increasing rent
Special benefits offered to pet owners

paragraphs.text: The roof terrace, laundry room, and ground-floor courtyard are available to every resident. Personal belongings should not be left in these places after use, since they may block access or create a safety risk. Tables on the terrace cannot be reserved by one household for an entire day. Residents are welcome to hold small gatherings, but they should leave the area clean and return furniture to its original position. Shared facilities remain pleasant only when each user considers the people arriving next.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on considerate behavior and fair access in shared apartment spaces.

paragraphs.text: Water leaks, electrical faults, and damage to common areas should be reported through the maintenance form as soon as they are noticed. The building team is responsible for fixed systems and equipment supplied with the apartment. Residents must replace ordinary light bulbs and repair damage caused by their own actions or guests. An emergency number is available when waiting until the next working day could cause harm. Clear reporting helps the manager decide both the urgency of a repair and who is responsible for it.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is how to report maintenance problems and distinguish building duties from resident responsibility.

paragraphs.text: Harbour Apartments observes quiet hours from ten at night until seven in the morning. During this period, music and conversations should not be audible from neighbouring homes. Visitors may stay overnight, but residents remain responsible for their behaviour in corridors and shared areas. Larger gatherings should end before quiet hours begin. These rules are not intended to prevent normal living; they provide a common standard for handling sound and guests in a building where many households live close together.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph sets expectations for quiet hours, gatherings, and responsibility for visitors.

paragraphs.text: Separate bins for food waste, recyclable packaging, and general rubbish are located beside each entrance. Large items must be taken to the collection room rather than left near the bins. Motion sensors reduce unnecessary lighting in corridors, while residents can monitor their own heating use through the building portal. The management publishes a monthly summary but does not identify individual apartments. The aim is to make responsible disposal and lower energy use convenient parts of everyday apartment life.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes practical waste sorting and energy-saving systems in the building.

passageTitle: Changes to the City Bus Network
instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: Short-term route changes during street repairs
Getting current travel information before a journey
Improvements for passengers with limited mobility
A simpler way to pay across different services
A permanent expansion into distant towns
New restrictions on carrying bicycles

paragraphs.text: Road work on Market Street will affect routes 4, 9, and 16 for approximately three weeks. Buses will use River Road and serve temporary stops marked with yellow signs. The diversion may add up to ten minutes during busy periods, although the normal timetable will remain in place. Stops inside the closed section will not be served. Once the work is complete, every affected route will return to Market Street, so passengers should not treat the temporary stop locations as a permanent network change.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains temporary diversions and stops caused by a limited period of road work.

paragraphs.text: Passengers can check live departure times through the CityMove application or the electronic displays at major stops. The application also sends alerts when a route is diverted or a journey is cancelled. Printed timetables remain useful for planning, but they cannot reflect an unexpected delay. Travellers without a smartphone may call the information line, which operates from early morning until midnight. Using a current source shortly before departure is the best way to avoid waiting for a service that has changed.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph compares ways to obtain up-to-date service information before travelling.

paragraphs.text: Twenty new buses will enter service this year with low floors and wider entrance areas. Each vehicle has a clearly marked space for a wheelchair or mobility scooter, and the next stop is announced both visually and by audio. Drivers are receiving additional training on positioning the bus close to the curb. Not every older stop is fully accessible yet, but the transport authority is publishing a map so passengers can plan journeys using stops that already meet the improved standard.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is a group of vehicle, driver, and information improvements for accessible travel.

paragraphs.text: A contactless CityPass can now be used on buses, trams, and the local ferry. Passengers tap the same card at the start of each journey, and the system automatically limits the total daily charge. Existing paper tickets will remain available for single trips during the transition. Users should register a card online if they want to protect its balance when it is lost. The change is intended to remove the need to understand a separate payment method for each type of transport.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on one integrated payment card that works across several transport services.

instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: Finding a starting level that matches current ability
Course formats designed around adult schedules
Showing progress through useful practical tasks
Support that connects learning with future plans
Automatic recognition as a university degree
Receiving a full refund after any class

paragraphs.text: Applicants are encouraged to attend a short advice session before enrolling in language, computing, or mathematics courses. An adviser asks about previous study and may suggest a simple placement activity. The aim is not to reject learners but to prevent them from choosing a class that repeats familiar material or moves too quickly. Someone with uneven skills may be offered a trial lesson before making a final choice. Starting at a suitable level gives learners a realistic challenge and a clearer path to progress.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes advice and placement used to select an appropriate starting level.

paragraphs.text: Most programmes are offered in more than one format because adult learners often balance study with work or family responsibilities. Evening classes provide weekly contact with a teacher, while blended courses combine monthly workshops with online activities. A small number of intensive weekend options are available for people who cannot attend regularly. The content and expected study time remain similar across formats. Learners should therefore choose according to when they can participate consistently, not because one option appears easier than another.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on flexible delivery formats designed for adults with different schedules.

paragraphs.text: Assessment is based mainly on tasks that reflect how a skill will be used outside the classroom. Computing learners create a document and solve common software problems, while language learners complete a conversation and a short written message. Teachers also give informal feedback during the course so students can improve before the final task. A practical assessment may feel less familiar than a traditional examination, but it demonstrates whether learners can apply their knowledge rather than simply remember isolated facts.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is evaluating progress through practical tasks that demonstrate real use of a skill.

paragraphs.text: Learners can arrange individual meetings with a study coach when motivation, time management, or future choices become difficult. The careers team also explains how course skills relate to local jobs and what further qualifications may be useful. This support is optional and does not replace classroom teaching. Its purpose is to help adults connect a current course with a longer plan, particularly when they are returning to education after many years or considering a change in employment.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes coaching and careers support that links present study with future goals.
